comments:
Summary and contributions:
This paper studies convex regularizers obtained as the Lovász extensions of a class of non-negative submodular set functions that vanish on the empty and full sets (including symmetric submodular functions). The main conceptual contribution is to show that these Lovász extensions are the convex envelopes of combinatorial functions that depend on all level sets of a vector (rather than on supports), thereby yielding convex penalties that encourage piecewise-constant solutions / specific level-set structures. The paper provides: (i) characterization of faces/extreme points of the induced unit ball and the allowed constant/level sets; (ii) examples linking classical total variation, cut functions, and new penalties based on order statistics and robust/noisy cuts; (iii) optimization strategies (proximal operators via SFM, minimum-norm-point, parametric max-flow for cuts, closed-form for cardinality-based functions) and an extension showing composition with an ℓ1 penalty; (iv) recovery guarantees for level-set estimation in a denoising (orthogonal design) setting, with detailed discussion of the staircase phenomenon for 1D TV and limitations in 2D.

Strengths:
- Novel conceptual link between level-set priors and submodular analysis; framing Lovász extensions as convex envelopes of max over level sets is elegant and clarifies what structure these penalties enforce.
- Unifies and generalizes several known regularizers (TV, fully connected pairwise clustering losses) and proposes interesting new penalties (order-statistics-based, noisy cuts) with plausible applications (clustering, outlier detection, robust change-point detection).
- Solid theoretical development: characterization of faces, conditions for agglomerative proximal paths, and a reasonably detailed level-set recovery theorem with interpretable conditions and probability bounds.
- Clear discussion of computational strategies and when efficient algorithms exist (cuts, cardinality-based), plus pointers to general-purpose approaches when needed.
- Proof sketches and appendices provided for main propositions and theorems (as included in the text provided).

Weaknesses and limitations:
- Computational scalability: many of the proposed methods rely on (potentially many) submodular function minimizations (SFM) or the minimum-norm-point algorithm. The paper acknowledges the worst-case high complexity (generic SFM algorithms can be expensive). More discussion and empirical evaluation of scalability for moderately large p would be helpful, as practical adoption depends on this.
- Empirical evaluation appears limited in the provided text to synthetic illustrations. Stronger empirical validation on larger-scale or real-world tasks (e.g., image denoising/segmentation for TV variants, clustering/outlier detection benchmarks for order-statistics penalties, change-point detection with robust cuts) would strengthen the paper and demonstrate practical utility.
- Some of the recovery conditions (orthogonal design / denoising setting) are restrictive; while this is a standard first step, guidance on how these results might extend to correlated designs or high-dimensional regression would be valuable.
- Certain technical conditions (e.g., condition (3) involving η_j, and behavior in 2D where η_j can be negative) limit guarantees; more intuition and guidance on model selection (how to choose F, λ, or weights) in practice would help practitioners.
- Presentation is dense and technical; while rigorous, some sections (e.g., proofs relying on prior deep results from submodular polyhedral theory) may be hard to parse for readers unfamiliar with submodular analysis. A few more illustrative examples and algorithm pseudo-code would improve accessibility.

Soundness:
The presented results appear sound and are supported by proofs (or proof sketches) that build on established submodular analysis (Lovász extension, base polyhedron properties). I find the arguments plausible and consistent; where complexity claims are made they are qualified appropriately. Given the dependence on classical submodular machinery, a reader should be able to verify details by consulting the cited references.

Suggestions for improvement:
- Add more extensive experiments, including real datasets and runtime/scale evaluations, to demonstrate practical feasibility and to compare to alternative structured penalties.
- Provide practical guidance for choosing submodular functions F and regularization parameters (λ), and discuss heuristics or approximations when exact SFM is too costly.
- Include algorithm pseudo-code, complexity summaries, and memory requirements for the proximal methods in different cases (cuts, cardinality-based, generic SFM).
- Discuss possible extensions beyond orthogonal designs and give intuition/limitations for correlated predictors.

Overall recommendation:
This is a strong, original, and well-motivated theoretical contribution that extends the reach of submodular-based convex regularizers to level-set structure. The work is likely to be of interest to researchers in convex optimization, structured sparsity, and imaging/clustering communities. With additional empirical validation and more guidance on practical computation, the impact would be higher. I recommend a positive assessment.
